data.title: Eastern Company: A Stronger Q1 Bodes Well For The Rest Of The Year
data.description:
Introduction

I have been keeping an eye on the Eastern Company (NASDAQ:EML) for several years now, but the share price hasn’t exactly performed well. The company has now also reoriented itself as it decided to sell the Big 3 Mold division in the third quarter of last year, and that division was recently included in the financial statements as a "discontinued operation" before Eastern sold the equipment, workforce and customer list of the ISBM division of Big 3 Precision mold portion of the company.

The Eastern Company will retain the other Big 3 brand, which focuses on Precision Products, which produces turnkey returnable packaging that is used in the assembly process of vehicles and aircraft. The Eberhard division focuses on the development and manufacturing of access and security hardware (like latches and locks) while Velvac manufactures a vision technology for OEMs (for instance mirror cameras and LED indicators incorporated in a truck’s side mirror) and is a provider of aftermarket components to the heavy-duty truck market in North America.

Decent earnings and a decent cash flow

In the first quarter of the current financial year, the company reported total revenue of $63.3M. That’s a 2% decrease compared to the same quarter of last year, but this is related to the Big 3 Mold business, which now has the status of a discontinued operation, which means its Q1 revenue of almost $4M was removed from Eastern’s consolidated top-line result. This means that on a like-for-like basis, Eastern’s revenue would have increased.

As the income statement below shows, the gross margin decreased while the operating profit also came in just over 5% lower than in the same quarter a year ago. This difference can once again be explained by the de-consolidation of Big 3 Mold, which generated an operating profit of approximately $0.2M in the first quarter of this year.

Looking at Eastern’s bottom-line result, the company reported net income of $1.9M from continuing operations and added approximately $37,000 in net profit from discontinued operations, resulting in a total consolidated net profit of $1.94M, a similar result compared to Q1 last year. The EPS in Q1 2025 was approximately $0.32 of which $0.31/share came from the continuing operations. That’s an increase of one cent per share due to the slightly higher net income in combination with a lower share count, as Eastern is buying back its own shares.

The income statement was also negatively impacted by a change in the carrying amount of right-of-use assets. As you can see below, Eastern Company reported a negative operating cash flow of $1.85M, but this is caused by a relatively hefty amount of cash required for its working capital elements. Adjusted for those working capital changes, the underlying operating cash flow was approximately $7.2M and roughly $7.1M after also taking the lease expenses into account.

The capex was very low at $0.85M, which means Eastern Company generated $6.25M in free cash flow on an underlying basis. That’s in excess of $1/share.

While the company’s underlying free cash flow was quite strong in the first quarter of the year, we can’t just extrapolate this to calculate the full-year free cash flow. Last year, for instance, the full-year capex was closer to $10M for the year while the normalized depreciation and amortization expenses are currently around $6M per year. This means we should reasonably expect the full-year capex to come in higher than the Q1 annualized capex.

Investment thesis

I currently have no position in Eastern Company, but I like the dedication of the new CEO who seems to determined to build Eastern out to a leader in its sector, with a focus on margins. Fortunately, the balance sheet remains pretty strong with a positive working capital position of in excess of $65M while the net debt level is just over $33M (excluding lease liabilities). This means the company has options (and time) to improve its financial performance, but the net income and net free cash flow on an underlying basis was already encouraging.

I’d like to see confirmation of the stronger than expected Q1 result in the next few quarters, so for now, I’m still just a "hold" on the stock.

data.title: FX: Risk Premium To Stay In The Dollar
data.description:
By Chris Turner

While we’re not expecting the dollar to collapse, we do think there are enough negative factors out there to keep it under pressure for the rest of the year.

Divestment alert

There has been a lot of talk about the ‘Sell America’ thesis and also ‘de-dollarization’. The two are linked, but the former, in our opinion, best describes the acute sell-off in US asset markets in April. The latter represents the much slower, secular trend away from a unipolar to a multipolar global financial system.

Relevant for both is the view that foreigners will end up holding fewer US assets than they have previously. A key focal point here is the US Treasury market and any evidence that foreigners have indeed been selling. There will therefore be interest in the US TIC data released on 18 June, which covers the month of April. Were central banks one of the key communities selling Treasuries that month?

The chart below shows foreign official holdings of Treasuries, drawing on data from both the US Treasury TIC report and the Fed's custody holdings. The latter is a subset of the former, but they typically profile quite well. The more frequent release from the Fed shows a $20bn decline in foreign official holdings since April and suggests the April TIC data could well support the foreign divestment hypothesis – a dollar negative.

Snapshot of foreign central bank holdings of Treasuries

The macro is dollar negative too

We expect tariff and US policy uncertainty to keep a risk premium in the dollar this year. One such policy on the market’s radar is Section 899 of the tax bill, which threatens a new withholding tax on foreign investors whose governments are deemed to run discriminatory tax regimes. Should that go into law, expect the dollar to turn quite offered into year-end as global investors optimize portfolios ahead of its introduction in 2026.

But dollar weakness will not be entirely down to a risk premium. The softer US growth trajectory and the restart of the Fed's easing cycle later this year should keep the dollar offered. Fed rate cuts, in particular, can reduce dollar hedging costs and make it easier to increase hedge ratios on holdings of US assets. On this subject, we suspect that current high dollar interest rates are discouraging some of that activity.

Equally, the eurozone story should start to look a little more attractive through 2026 as the benefits of this year’s fiscal stimulus decisions bear fruit. Importantly, we’re looking for eurozone money market rates to start picking up in late 2026 ahead of what could be an ECB hike in early 2027. We expect EUR/USD to trade towards the upper end of a 1.15-1.20 range at that stage. And that’s why this month we are nudging our EUR/USD forecast profile slightly higher.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: TSPA ETF: Tracking S&P 500 With A Higher Expense Ratio
data.description:
What is TSPA?

The T. Rowe Price US Equity Research ETF (NYSEARCA:TSPA) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as ETF) that invests in mostly large-cap American listed stocks. T. Rowe is pitching that they have the potential to outperform the broad market S&P 500 index, using a disciplined portfolio construction process whereby it weights each sector approximately the same as the S&P 500 Index, while actively picking winners. A team of industry-focused T. Rowe Price equity analysts is directly responsible for selecting stocks for the fund, as they attempt to beat the S&P 500 benchmark.

I recently gave this ETF a Hold rating given its underperformance versus other low fee competitors like SPY and VOO. The fund has had similar returns to SPY since Liberation Day, but has also charged investors higher fees than other peers like SPY or Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO). For now, I'll keep TSPA as a Hold as it doesn't have enough differentiation to warrant its offering as a stand alone actively managed ETF - but keep an eye on it, since the fund is a newer offering with only four years of history. TSPA's benchmark target is the S&P 500 (SPY) and this article will tell you whether it's worth investing with T Rowe's asset managers or whether you should just buy SPY, which is also an ETF, but is passively managed with lower fees. That means its fund managers don't make many trades, and only rebalance every once in a while, versus T. Rowe's managers, who have the mandate to buy and sell stocks more often if they feel that there's a compelling investment opportunity.

I also recently wrote about another similar fund from T. Rowe with the same benchmark, TCHP, that has underperformed for years. After further review, my analysis shows that TSPA is not worth an investment right now, but is worth keeping an eye on as it's actively managed and the fund managers may look to increase their portfolio range & diversity given these similar results to passively managed ETF's with the same benchmark.

Fund Breakdown

TSPA was launched on June 8, 2021 by T. Rowe with a mandate to create a fund that provided long term capital growth. T. Rowe noted in their prospectus to investors that the strategy attempts to create a portfolio with similar characteristics to the S&P 500 Stock Index with the potential to provide excess returns relative to the Index. They also note that "the fund attempts to outperform the Index by overweighting those stocks that are viewed favorably relative to their weighting in the Index, and underweighting or avoiding those stocks that are viewed negatively" which is essentially stating that they will have slightly larger positions in big companies like Apple Inc. (AAPL) and Nvidia Inc. (NVDA), as they are still constrained to keep sector weightings in line. Ultimately, T. Rowe is basically saying that they are launching a fund to track the S&P 500, and be more actively managed to generate additional alpha from those returns, by utilizing enhanced concentration on holdings they find are the best bets in each sector.

But upon further review of the ETF's results, I'm not really optimistic that the fund has achieved what it set out to do. Below is a chart over the past year and since Liberation Day showing almost identical returns to SPY. Why bother with this actively managed, undifferentiated strategy with higher fees?

The fund employs a 0.34% yearly ETF expense ratio for the fees to the asset manager. For context, SPY's expense ratio fee is 0.09%, almost four times smaller than TSPA. With limited differences to SPY, I wonder if this ETF is any different than other competitors like BlackRock's (IVV) and passive Vanguard offerings. For now, given the slight underperformance and limited history, I'd wait on the sidelines before parking my money here.

Previous Results & Fund Portfolio

When comparing TSPA to the S&P 500, the portfolio greatly resembles SPY, with top holdings like AAPL, NVDA and MSFT and sector diversification mostly in line with the benchmark. Some of the big techs have slightly higher weightings, but ultimately, investors could simply hold SPY without paying a 0.34% fee yearly, because this fund doesn't even look actively managed. Over the long term, if the fund philosophy remains unchanged, I'm not sure how much alpha can be generated with such miniscule differences in the portfolio; though to be fair, its alpha is 1.15 which is decent, but most investors expect >1.2% for actively managed funds.

Risk Profile

From a risk perspective, the fund has a beta of 1. A beta of 1 notes that the fund has the same volatility as the benchmark - and that has borne out in past results. Even though there was some minor outperformance, the recent down periods were down more than the benchmark - leading to a net effect of no change to the beta. Additionally, the Sharpe ratio measures risk-adjusted return, helping investors determine if they are being fairly compensated for the level of risk they are taking. The formula is the portfolio return minus the risk-free return, divided by the standard deviation. Essentially, it tracks the return required by investors for the risk they are taking, and it should be above 1 to indicate acceptable returns for the risk taken. The higher, the better.

Given that 0.68 is above 0, but below 1, it indicates that the fund is generating some, but not much, excess return relative to its risk. I suppose that if you are comfortable with a bit more volatility, then you can pick this fund, but there are other offerings with lower expense and better Sharpe ratios that track SPY just as closely... I don't see much need for this offering.

Bottom Line

The bottom line for this fund, in my opinion, is that it's a fine option, but I'm not convinced it's a need for investors. It hasn't outperformed SPY since liberation day like TCHP, another actively managed fund that tracks the S&P 500 and I don't think I can justify paying a 0.34% expense ratio compared to 0.09% for SPY or 0.03% for the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) and the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV).

This ETF has limited differentiation, no difference in beta, and hasn't outperformed its benchmark. There are many alternatives to buy when trying to track and outperform the S&P 500 as an investor, but I'll wait and see its performance over the next few years before anointing it a worthy actively managed ETF. If investors prefer strong outperformers in actively managed ETF's with large cap equity benchmarks, try the Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO), iShares Core S&P U.S. Growth ETF (IUSG) or Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG).

data.title: PGIM Jennison Global Infrastructure Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
MARKET REVIEW

The S&P Global Infrastructure Index (the Index) advanced 4.4% in the first quarter. The Index’s performance was led higher by strong performance in the Utility sector. The midstream Energy sector also saw strong performance for the quarter. The Transportation sector also saw positive performance for the period.

QUARTER

The PGIM Jennison Global Infrastructure Fund (MUTF:PGJAX) advanced but modestly underperformed the S&P Global Infrastructure Index for the quarter.

In absolute terms, the Fund experienced gains across most of its sectors. Strong performance in both the Transportation sector (Aena SME SA, Tokyo Metro, Waste Connections, Ferrovial SE) and the Utility sector (Entergy, E.ON SE, Enel SpA, CenterPoint Energy, NiSource, CMS Energy, National Grid PLC, Southern Company, PPL) helped lead the portfolio higher for the quarter. Gains in the Energy sector (Williams Companies, Targa Resources, Cheniere Energy) also helped absolute performance for the period. However, declines in several Utility stocks (Sempra, PG&E, Constellation Energy, Vistra, Drax Group) hurt absolute performance for the quarter.

Relative to the S&P Global Infrastructure Index, the Fund was hurt by stock selection in the Utility sector. An underweight and stock selection in the midstream Energy sector also hurt performance relative to the Index. Strong stock selection in the Transportation sector helped to offset much of the underperformance.

KEY CONTRIBUTORS

  • Aena (ANYYY, AENA)
  • American Tower Corporation (AMT)
  • Entergy Corporation (ETR)

Aena (AENA) owns and operates approximately 46 airports in Spain including duty-free and specialty stores, restaurant services, and car rentals located within its airports. AENA continues to see solid demand growth for leisure travel and retail spending in its core Spanish and global markets driven by low-cost carrier growth. AENA has consistently outperformed guidance over the last 2 years driving positive earnings revisions. The company continues to return capital to shareholders with an 80% payout ratio, supporting an attractive dividend yield. Valuation remains below average.

American Tower Corporation (AMT) is the world’s largest independent owner, operator and developer of wireless towers and also owns a highly interconnected footprint of U.S. data center facilities. After performing poorly during 4Q24, shares of AMT rebounded during 1Q25 primarily due to positive sentiment related to 1) the steady decline in U.S. interest rates during the quarter, 2) Foreign Exchange tailwinds related to its non-U.S. asset portfolio.

Entergy Corporation (ETR) engages in the production and retail distribution of electricity in the United States. After meaningfully outperforming its regulated Utility peers in 4Q24, ETR continued its strong performance in 1Q25 after posting 4Q24 earnings higher than market expectations and once again raising the capital expenditures outlook and earnings guidance for the company through 2028.

KEY DETRACTORS

  • PG&E (PCG)
  • Digital Realty Trust (DLR)
  • Sempra (SRE)

PG&E (PCG) is a San Francisco, CA, based energy holding company that generates, transmits and distributes electricity. The company produces power using nuclear, hydro, fuel cell, solar and fossil fuel sources, while serving customers through its main operating subsidiary, Pacific Gas and Electric. PCG shares underperformed in 1Q25 as the Los Angeles fires shook investor confidence regarding California wildfire risk, despite the fires not occurring near PG&E’s service territory and with financial protections in place per the 2019 legislation. While we believe PCG’s wildfire mitigation investments have proved effective in northern California, and the industry seems to have a constructive relationship with the state government, the regulatory/legislative process to further enhance protections for investor-owned utilities will take time and will likely be volatile.

Digital Realty Trust (DLR) brings companies and data together by delivering the full spectrum of data center, colocation, and interconnection solutions. During the quarter, DLR reported 4Q24 results which included a lower percentage of bookings from generative artificial intelligence (AI) workloads than the previous quarter due to fewer large scale (>1 MegaWatt) signings during the quarter. This datapoint exacerbated a selloff in the Digital Realty Trust and other AI-related stocks that was already underway due to the January 2025 DeepSeek news.

We believe the weakness in the company’s shares is overdone and fears of a significant near-term pullback in leasing activity for large scale powered data center capacity are misguided; we continue to believe DLR is well-positioned to benefit from the strong long- term outlook for data center capacity demand from cloud computing and generative AI.

Sempra (SRE) operates as an energy infrastructure company in the United States and internationally. It operates through three segments: Sempra California, Sempra Texas Utilities, and Sempra Infrastructure. The company’s shares performed poorly during the quarter as management negatively surprised investors with a >10% cut to its 2025-2026 earnings per share (EPS) guidance due to 1) timing considerations surrounding the Sempra Texas rate review which drove an acceleration of capex and subsequent degradation of earned return on equity (ROEs); and 2) higher interest and Operations & Maintenance (O&M) costs across the board, on top of higher equity dilution to fund increased capex. While we are disappointed in this poorly communicated reset of expectations for near-term earnings, we remain positive on Sempra’s longer-term earnings growth potential, especially at Sempra Texas.

PORTFOLIO OUTLOOK

Overall, listed infrastructure stocks performed well during 1Q25 relative to broad global equity indices as several factors, including rising concerns about the Trump administration’s escalating tariff policies causing a potential recession in the U.S., contributed to investors rotating into “safe haven” securities such as U.S. Treasuries and defensive equities such as U.S. regulated utilities and wireless towers.

Expansionary fiscal policy shifts signaled by several European nations including Germany, most notably as a response to the mounting trade war initiated by escalating U.S. tariffs fueled investor optimism in a stimulus-driven recovery in Europe’s economic activity. Such optimism supported a 1Q 2025 rally in European transport infrastructure companies, particularly those with construction operations, and European electric utilities and power generators that can benefit from accelerating demand growth.

North American natural gas-focused energy stocks rallied throughout the quarter in sympathy with strength in the Henry Hub natural gas forward curve as investors responded to improved sentiment regarding accelerating long term demand growth for natural gas. Additionally, an innovative $1.6 billion gas pipeline and power generation project by Williams (WMB) to power a data center under a 10-year contract served to buoy investor sentiment for natural gas infrastructure stocks during the quarter.

After a strong start to the year, power generation companies as well as data center stocks performed relatively poorly during Q1 2025 as DeepSeek, a Chinese AI company, triggered a meaningful selloff in AI-related stocks. In January 2025, DeepSeek unveiled an innovative reasoning model that raised investor concerns that DeepSeek’s reportedly low-cost approach could significantly reduce demand for the most advanced AI hardware and, by extension, reduce demand for highly electricity-consumptive data center capacity. This sharp reversal in investor sentiment regarding AI-related stocks served as a strong headwind for U.S.-based power generators and data center companies who had rallied throughout 2024 as key beneficiaries of strong demand due to accelerating AI- related capex.

We believe the Funds electric utility, power generation, and renewable energy holdings are well-positioned to capitalize upon a projected upward inflection in electricity demand growth driven by rapidly rising data center power needs, manufacturing/industrial re- shoring, continued electric vehicle adoption, and further electrification of a broad array of industries. Based on the team’s channel checks with AI industry participants, we believe the selloff in U.S. power generator stocks triggered by the unveiling of China’s DeepSeek AI reasoning model in January 2025 was overdone; the outlook for AI-related infrastructure capex continues to look very robust and we anticipate a reduction in regulatory and legislative uncertainty in 2H25 regarding colocation of large data centers with power plants and transmission cost allocation could serve as a positive catalyst for U.S. power generator stocks. Additionally, we view increased focus on electric grid reliability and resiliency from regulators and policymakers as supportive for sustaining robust levels of regulated rate-base investment which we anticipate will further support attractive EPS growth for most regulated electric utilities in the U.S. and Western Europe through 2030 and likely beyond.

In PGIM’s view, North American natural gas infrastructure will play a critical role in supplying the new gas-fired power plants that will likely be required to serve the power demand growth dramatically accelerating through 2030 and beyond; we believe several new proposed natural gas pipeline projects announced in 2H24 and early 2025 corroborate this optimistic outlook. We remain selective with the Fund’s energy infrastructure exposure with a focus on strategic natural gas infrastructure networks, strong operational and financial execution that translates into attractive levels of free cash flow, and a healthy return of capital to shareholders through dividend increases and/or share repurchases.

The North American toll road market stands to benefit from solid demand for new projects, while existing assets are displaying attractive returns and pricing power. In Europe, the improved fiscal policy support in Germany for infrastructure spending appears to be a tailwind for concession companies that maintain contracting businesses. We still see favorable growth in select European airports where traffic is recovering alongside retail growth.

North American rails stand to benefit from improved price/cost dynamics, but near-term volume uncertainty tied to tariff risks are a headwind leading to a selective approach targeting names with company specific catalysts. Finally, we remain constructive on U.S. waste stocks which continue to exhibit defensive characteristics and strong pricing power.

Lastly, regarding communications infrastructure, we favor exposure to wireless towers given their recession-resistant business models, including inflation adjusted revenues. Additionally, we believe data centers are well-positioned to capitalize on robust demand growth from existing cloud data services and growing contributions from generative AI.

Barron’s: PGIM Investments ranked 4 out of 48, 8 out of 47, 12 out of 46 firms for the 1-, 5-, and 10-year periods ended 12/31/2024, respectively. See back page for methodology which takes into account Lipper rankings. PGIM Jennison Global Infrastructure Fund (Class Z) Lipper total return ranking for the 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods as of 12/31/2024 for the Global Infrastructure Fund category were: 62/97, 79/97, 60/95, and 23/64, respectively. Lipper Funds category rankings are based on total return, do not take sales charges into account, and are calculated against all funds in each fund’s respective Lipper category. Lipper total return ranking for the 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods as of 3/31/2025: 49/86, 35/77, 24/71, and 22/45, respectively. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.
Since
AVERAGE ANNUAL RETURN AS OF 3/31/2025 YTD 1-year 3-year 5-year 10-year 09/25/2013
Total Returns (without sales charges)
PGIM Jennison Global Infrastructure Fund Z 4.05 13.77 3.54 10.68 5.91 7.26
S&P Global Infrastructure Index 4.41 17.75 5.15 12.87 5.55 6.16
SEC Standardized Returns (with sales charges)
PGIM Jennison Global Infrastructure Fund Z — 13.77 3.54 10.68 5.91 7.26

Source: PGIM Inc. Past performance does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the past performance data quoted. Investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than original cost. For the most recent month-end performance go to PGIM Investments.

Gross operating expense: Class Z, 1.73%. Net operating expense: Class Z, 1.17%, after contractual reduction through 2/28/2026. Expenses are as of the most recent prospectus. Net operating expenses reflect expenses after fee waivers and/or expense reimbursements by PGIM Investments, if any. The contractual reduction date is the date through which PGIM Investments has agreed to waive fees or reimburse expenses, if applicable. Expenses for the current year may exceed the net operating expenses listed above due to exclusions from any applicable contractual waiver or reimbursement, which may fluctuate. PGIM Investments may recoup certain waived fees or reimbursed expenses. See the prospectus for more information.

Class Z shares may be available to group retirement plans and institutional investors through certain retirement, mutual fund wrap and asset allocation programs. They may also be available to institutional investors and through fee- or commission-based retail brokerage programs of certain financial intermediaries. Class Z shares are generally closed to new retirement plans. Please see the prospectus for additional information about fees, expenses, and investor eligibility.

Annualized returns without sales charges describe the return to the investor before any sales charges are imposed. SEC standardized return describes the return to the investor after maximum sales charges are imposed. All returns assume share price changes, as well as the compounding effect of reinvested dividends and capital gains. Returns may reflect fee waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Without such, returns would be lower. Performance by share class may vary. The performance data featured represents past performance for a period of less than one year. While past performance is never an indication of future results, short periods of performance may be particularly unrepresentative of long-term performance for certain types of funds.

Risks of investing in the fund include but are not limited to the following: The Fund focuses its investments in infrastructure companies, thereby making the Fund more susceptible to adverse economic, social, political, and regulatory occurrences affecting the industries of infrastructure companies. Foreign securities are subject to currency fluctuations and political uncertainty. Emerging market investments are subject to greater volatility and price declines. Equity and equity-related securities may be subject to changes in value, and their values may be more volatile than those of other asset classes. The Fund’s blend investment style may subject the Fund to the risks of both value and growth investing. Small- and Mid-cap investments may be subject to more erratic market movements than large-cap stocks. Master limited partnerships are subject to complex accounting, tax and valuation issues. Investments are subject to limited control, voting rights, and liquidity, as well as risks related to potential conflicts of interest, dilution and cash flow. The general partner may force sales at undesirable times or prices. Real estate companies and Real estate investment trusts (REITS) may be leveraged, which increases risk. REIT performance depends on the strength of the real estate markets, REIT management and property management which can be affected by many factors, including national and regional economic conditions. There is no guarantee the Fund’s objective will be achieved. Risks are more fully explained in the fund’s prospectus.

The following are the top 10 holdings by percentage of total net assets as of 03/31/2025: NextEra Energy Inc (4.77%), The Southern Co (4.45%), Ferrovial SE (4.22%), Aena SME SA (3.68%), American Tower Corp (3.52%), NiSource Inc (3.45%), Elia Group SA/NV (3.37%), Entergy Corp (3.32%), National Grid PLC (3.05%) and E.ON SE (3.04%). Holdings are subject to change.

S&P Global Infrastructure Index is an unmanaged index that consists of 75 companies from around the world that represent the listed infrastructure universe. To create diversified exposure across the global listed infrastructure market, the index has balanced weights across three distinct infrastructure clusters: Utilities, Transportation, and Energy. Standard & Poor’s 500 (S&P 500) Index is an unmanaged market capitalization-weighted index of 500 stocks of large U.S. companies.

An investment cannot be made directly in an index.

The views expressed herein are those of Jennison Associates investment professionals at the time the comments were made, may not be reflective of their current opinions, and are subject to change without notice. Neither the information contained herein nor any opinion expressed shall be construed to constitute investment advice or an offer to sell or a solicitation to buy any securities mentioned herein.

Barron’s: Used with permission. ©2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc. Source: Barron’s, Feb. 27, 2025. Barron’s rankings are based on asset-weighted returns in funds in five categories: U.S. Equity; World Equity; Mixed Asset; Taxable Bond; and Tax-Exempt (each a “Barron’s ranking category”). Rankings also take into account an individual fund’s performance within its Lipper peer universe. Lipper calculated each fund’s net total return for the year ended Dec. 31, 2024, minus the effects of 12b-1 fees and sales charges. Each fund in the survey was given a percentile ranking, with 100 the highest and 1 the lowest in its category. That ranking measured how a fund compared with its peer “universe,” as tracked by Lipper, not just the funds in the survey. Individual fund scores were then multiplied by the 2024 weighting of their Barron’s ranking category as determined by the entire Lipper universe of funds. Those fund scores were then totaled, creating an overall score and ranking for each fund family in the survey in each Barron’s ranking category. To qualify for the ranking, firms must offer at least three active mutual funds or actively run ETFs in Lipper’s general U.S. Stock category; one in World Equity; and one Mixed Asset. They also need to offer at least two taxable bond funds and one national tax-exempt bond fund. All funds must have a track record of at least one year.

Consider a fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses carefully before investing. The prospectus and summary prospectus contain this and other information about the fund. Contact your financial professional for a prospectus and summary prospectus. Read them carefully before investing.

Investment products are distributed by Prudential Investment Management Services LLC, member FINRA and SIPC. PGIM Investments is a registered investment advisor and investment manager to PGIM registered investment companies. Jennison Associates is a registered investment advisor. All are Prudential Financial affiliates. © 2025 Prudential Financial, Inc. and its related entities. PGIM, PGIM Investments, Jennison Associates, Jennison, and the PGIM logo are service marks of Prudential Financial, Inc. and its related entities, registered in many jurisdictions worldwide.

This material is being provided for informational or educational purposes only and does not take into account the investment objectives or financial situation of any client or prospective clients. The information is not intended as investment advice and is not a recommendation. Clients seeking information regarding their particular investment needs should contact their financial professional.

data.title: The AI Bubble: Advanced AI Faces A 'Complete Accuracy Collapse'
data.description:
The investment context

The Gen AI theme has been the primary force shaping the financial markets since Microsoft (MSFT) invested in OpenAI and ChatGPT in late 2022. The tech mega-caps companies associated with the Gen AI theme, primarily Nvidia (NVDA), but also Alphabet (GOOG), and Meta (META) count for over a third of the S&P 500 Index (SP500).

During this period, the valuations of these Gen AI mega-caps reached bubble levels, which consequently pushed the S&P 500 valuation near the historic highs based on many valuation ratios. For example, the Shiller P/E ratio is over 37.

At the center of the Gen AI bubble is an enormous AI capex, so large that it's affecting the U.S. GDP. These AI companies are actually clients of each other, so this Gen AI capex is eventually reflected in their profits.

However, we still don't know what the ROR on this Gen AI capex will be - and it could all be a wasted investment if the Gen AI proves to be a fad technology.

At this point, the application of Gen AI is widely used in agentic AI, where the Gen AI performs some simple tasks. Yet, we still don't see major productivity effects on the broader economy.

How Does Gen AI Work?

A simple way of explaining how Gen AI works is basically:

  • you need to have a large data set,
  • you train a Large Language Model on that dataset
  • the large language model, or LLM, is then able to perform tasks learned from that dataset, for example create lyrics, music, images, computer code, drawings, etc.

The early excitement about Gen AI was the ability of AI to understand human language, and reply in human language with useful information, and especially the ability to generate images.

However, for Gen AI to be able to perform complex tasks, necessary to increase productivity, the AI must be able to start reasoning - beyond the data set training. In other words, the AI needs to become like a human, to think like a human. That's the higher level of AI - and the entire success on Gen AI technology depends on the ability to reach that level defined as the Artificial General Intelligence AGI.

In simple terms - that would be a robot, or a humanoid able to perform all complex tasks, like we see in sci-tech movies.

The industry moved towards AGI with Open AI o1 model in 2024, but the real breakthrough was the DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) R1 model in January 2025, which outperformed all other models, as a fraction of the cost.

Obviously, if the reasoning models progress and reach the AGI stage, the AI capex could prove to be profitable, but human society will fundamentally change forever. These reasoning AI models will replace human scientists - and all future innovations will be created by AGI.

The Apple's angle

One major company that has been lagging in the development of Gen AI models is Apple (AAPL). Yes, Apple adopted Apple Intelligence, but even this largely failed.

So, Apple is a curious case. Why is Apply lagging in Gen AI, given the company's record of innovations?

The "Complete Accuracy Collapse" study

Apple has just released a study titled "The Illusion of Thinking: Understanding the Strengths and Limitations of Reasoning Models via the Lens of Problem Complexity," in which Apple finds that the Advanced Reasoning AI suffers from a "complete accuracy collapse" when facing a highly complex problem.

This study essentially finds that the current AI models are nowhere near AGI. In fact, apparently current LRMs are just good at memorizing the training dataset, but completely break down when facing a complex task that would require human-like thinking. In fact, even the best LRMs quit when unable to find a solution.

Yes, LRM models have been tested for Ph.D. level intelligence by solving very complex math problems. But Apple claims that these tests were corrupt, where solutions have been hidden and easily accessible by AI.

The Apple study tests the LRM models using puzzles.

  • The study finds that LLM models are able to solve a low complexity puzzle.
  • When facing a medium level puzzle, advanced reasoning LRMs outperform basic LLMs.
  • However, when facing a complex puzzle, both LLMs and advanced LRMs have a "complete collapse of accuracy". Specifically, the "reasoning" completely breaks down, not only by giving wrong answers, but also by quitting the problem.

So, what are the implications of the Apple study?

The key implication is that the advanced reasoning Gen AI is not close to the AGI, and that the progress has hit the wall. This means that the Gen AI capex is very likely a wasted investment.

With respect to Apple's lagging in Gen AI, it seems like Apple justifies a careful approach to AI by stating that AGI might not be reachable. On the other hand, some might look at this study as Apple's excuse for lagging in Gen AI.

Either way, Apple's study must be replicated, and the reasoning AI models must be retested using Apple's approach to verify the results. At this point, there is no reason to suspect the conclusion that AGI is currently unreachable is biased or wrong.

Implications

The investment implications are obvious. The Gen AI is likely a bubble, which is eventually going to burst once the investors realize that the massive Gen AI capex is mostly a wasted investment. Specifically, the Gen AI technology is unlikely to reach the AGI stage, based on Apple's study, which means that Gen AI is unlikely to significantly boost productivity.

Thus, the S&P 500 bubble is likely to burst as the Gen AI bubble bursts. At this point investors are completely ignoring the warning from the Apple study, and the bubble could continue to inflate.

Regardless of short-term volatility, the S&P 500 is a Sell at these valuations, facing an eventual Gen AI bubble burst as more investors start questioning the validity of Apple's study, and a possible admission from related companies that AGI is currently unreachable.

data.title: PGIM Jennison Financial Services Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
MARKET REVIEW

Entering 2025, markets had benefited from a prolonged macroeconomic period that was supportive of equities, and especially for large-cap growth stocks. Economic data signaled a healthy market climate, the Federal Reserve (Fed) began a series of rate cuts as inflation had moderated, and there was some optimism around the potential effect of the second Trump administration’s economic policies. However, uncertainty was also rising.

While it was expected that President Trump’s agenda would shape the broader investment landscape this year, greater uncertainty around implementation of his policies—trade and tariffs, tax reform, immigration, etc.—created new risks. As new tariffs were announced during the first quarter of 2025, it was unclear as to the magnitude of the import levies and their use as a negotiating tool. Volatility increased given heightened concerns that higher input costs and supply chain disruptions would lead to higher prices and dampen consumer spending. Sentiment began to wane amidst a rising tide of uncertainty.

The surprise launch of China’s DEEPSEEK artificial intelligence model further pressured technology and AI-related companies (across all market caps and styles), key drivers of 2024’s market returns. By early March the market had surrendered all post- election gains. Defensive sectors outperformed during the quarter due to market turbulence, while cyclicals gained from anticipated policy support, notably in energy. Conversely, higher growth and higher valuation sectors declined and underperformed the broader market. Both the S&P 500 and Russell 1000 Growth indices posted their first negative quarter in almost two years, while Russell 1000 Value was up slightly.

QUARTER

The PGIM Jennison Financial Services Fund (MUTF:PFSAX) declined and underperformed the 2.9% return of the S&P Composite 1500 Financials Index during the quarter. Security selection within financial services (especially an overweight to Apollo Global Management) and capital markets (especially an overweight to KKR) detracted the most from relative results. On the upside, an overweight allocation to insurance and an underweight allocation to consumer finance added the most value.

KEY CONTRIBUTORS

  • Progressive Corp (PGR)
  • Marsh & McLennan (MMC)
  • Visa (V)

Progressive Corp is the second largest auto insurer in the U.S. The company has a strong track record of consistent growth, pricing discipline, risk management, and profit stability. Given its current profitability levels and operational excellence, we expect Progressive Corp to actively begin to accelerate growth through share gains and margin expansion. The stock outperformed because it executed well in the quarter, along with its defensive and high-quality characteristics being favored by investors.

Marsh & McLennan is professional services company, provides advisory services and insurance solutions to clients in the areas of risk, strategy, and people worldwide. It operates through Risk and Insurance Services, and Consulting segments. The stock outperformed because it executed well in the quarter, along with its defensive and high-quality characteristics being favored by investors.

Visa is another name in the Fund with a stable and durable business model, along with high barriers to entry. The stock outperformed because it executed well in the quarter, along with its defensive and high-quality characteristics being favored by investors.

KEY DETRACTORS

  • KKR & CO. Inc (KKR)
  • Apollo Global Management (APO)
  • PNC Financial Services Group (PNC)

KKR & CO. Inc. is one of the world’s largest alternative asset managers, with over $450 billion in assets under management. Pensions and endowments continue to increase their allocation to alternatives, tending to favor the largest and most successful alternative managers. KKR benefits from this trend and is also expanding distribution into the retail market. Alternative asset managers underperformed in the quarter due to macro tariff concerns and the current heightened volatility.

Apollo Global Management is a global alternative asset manager, which offers asset management and retirement services solutions. The Company’s asset management business is engaged in raising, investing, and managing funds, accounts, and other vehicles, on behalf of pension, endowment, and sovereign wealth funds. Alternative asset managers underperformed in the quarter due to macro tariff concerns and the current heightened volatility.

PNC Financial Services Group is one of the largest diversified financial services companies in the U.S. As a “smart operator”, PNC is a well-managed commercial and retail bank. The company advanced on better-than-expected profit and revenues, especially accelerating fee revenue. This super regional bank underperformed in the quarter due to potential credit issues from macro tariff concerns and the current heightened volatility.

PORTFOLIO OUTLOOK

As we look forward into 2025, challenges continue to intensify. On April 2nd, President Trump initiated a series of tariffs impacting countries exporting to the United States, building upon existing trade measures with nations such as Canada and Mexico. These new tariffs raise the overall weighted average to approximately 25%, a level unseen in nearly a century. This policy shift, more aggressive than anticipated, elicited a negative market reaction, with equity prices losing significant value following the announcement, signaling concerns about U.S. economic growth, and rising inflationary pressures.

The staggered deadlines for implementation of tariffs, which involve a phased rollout of duties on specific goods and trading partners, provide an opportunity for countries to negotiate; however, should negotiations fail, retaliatory measures, as demonstrated by China, are increasingly likely. The existing trade relationship with Mexico, now further strained, introduces additional vulnerabilities given the interconnectedness of supply chains.

Immediate concerns center on the potential impact on overall consumer demand, inflation, input costs, margins, and profits. The magnitude of this policy change requires careful monitoring of how our government’s actions will influence trading partners, as retaliatory measures could pose a significant headwind to economic expansion while simultaneously fueling inflation.

The administration’s aggressive and antagonistic approach to global trade is without precedent in our lifetimes. While acknowledging that the landscape is rapidly evolving, we are focused on navigating its complexities. We anticipate that economic growth will slow, with inflation likely to rise. Companies will likely face mounting pressure on profit margins, depressing earnings and valuation assumptions.

Against this evolving backdrop, we are integrating these dynamics into the Fund construction activity. We remain focused on identifying resilient opportunities in the face of the challenging trade landscape and resulting and fluid market dynamics.

AVERAGE ANNUAL RETURN AS OF 3/31/2025

data.title: Rates: Downside Limits For Yields, Vs. Bigger Room To The Upside
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA

How far can 10yr yields fall based off current thinking on interest rate cuts?

We’ve moved into calmer waters for market rates. The 10-year Treasury yield has recently tended not to stray too far from 4.5%. In the eurozone, the 10yr Euribor rate has trended around the 2.5% area, and the 10yr Bund yield is broadly flat to this.

If we were to map out a case for these rates to fall, we’d point to the rate-cutting tendency ahead as a key component. Here’s how that would go for the eurozone and the US:

  • In the eurozone, we anticipate that the European Central Bank will get to 1.75% (now 2.0%). The current 10yr rate is only 75bp above that future low. Perhaps we could squeeze a 25bp move lower. Realistically, the ECB would have to dip to 1.5% to make some room for a structurally lower 10yr rate. That, in fact, is the market discount. If that were to be realised, we could talk of 10yr rates dipping to the 2-2.25% area.
  • In the US, the Federal Reserve is expected to cut to the 3.25-3.5% range (now 4.25-4.5%). That would imply a curve of some 115bp to the current 10yr yield at 4.5%. That appears to present value in the 10yr yield. However, we need to account for the swap spread to 10yr SOFR, which cuts the "true curve" down to roughly 65bp (10yr SOFR now at around 4%). Maybe we could squeeze a 25bp move to the downside. To see a proper manoeuvre to the downside for the 10yr yield, the Fed would need to cut much more than is currently discounted.

The bottom line is that when we look at yields' capacity to fall, we find there are constraints. Yes, there is macro angst to consider, and a rate-cutting backdrop. But we'd need more than is currently discounted to rationalise meaningful falls in longer-tenor yields.

The other extreme - how much room is there for yields to rise?

In contrast, when we consider the potential for yields to rise, things begin to open up. The key ingredient on both sides of the Atlantic is the fiscal balance.

In the US, if the tax-cutting package currently going through Congress ultimately gets passed (and it looks like it will), it will leave the bond market with no plan in place to reduce the fiscal deficit. This piles the issuance pressure on as a theme. In the eurozone, plans for more spending (on defence "plus"; Germany in particular) won’t push average fiscal deficits to US-style extremes, but it will add to issuance pressures in a meaningful way, albeit likely more from 2026 onwards.

This has implications for how we see longer tenor market rates evolving through 2025/2026.

  • For the US, we see the passage of the tax-cutting bill plus a tariff-induced rise in inflation as themes for the third quarter, manifesting in upward pressure on the 10yr yield. We pitch 4.75% as a target, but would not be surprised to see this extend to 5%. Subsequently, we have a calming process as the Fed cuts, starting in the fourth quarter. And the 10yr yield eventually eases its way back down towards 4.25% (mostly through 2026).
  • For the eurozone, the bigger independent upside pressure for the 10yr Bund yield is through 2026 as the ECB finishes with cuts, and the aforementioned fiscal pressure builds. That coaxes the 10yr yield to the 2.75% area (and likely above).

Bigger upside to yields, and issuance pressure is the common denominator

On balance, we’re left with a bearish tilt to our forecasts for bond yields. The US front end can certainly be dragged lower should economic data disappoint in the coming few months (and quarters), but this should mostly serve to steepen the curve. The primary driver of longer-term yields comes from longer-term issuance pressure.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Nvidia: Will See You At $100 Soon Once Again (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Executive Summary

I am very happy that my bearish late-March Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) thesis aged well, because, as I predicted, the share price dipped below $100 in April, which was my target price. As I promised, I loaded up aggressively and I do hope that people who followed my plan enjoyed returns because it was a really nice bull that happened over just a couple of months. Nvidia remains fundamentally strong, but the valuation became inflated after the big recent bull run. Therefore, once again, I am reiterating a Strong Sell recommendation and will be looking for new opportunities to buy once the share price returns back closer to $100.

Why This AI Superstar is A Strong Sell?

I know that when readers see another Strong Sell rating for NVDA they might just say that it is another bear that is trying to hype. I want to say that I am not a bear, I actually love Nvidia a lot and it is one of the stocks that helped me to enjoy nice gains. In my previous analysis, apart from sharing a successful forecast that NVDA could hit the $100 target (which really happened), I also explained fundamental strength of NVDA.

As you can see from the share price chart, the stock dipped slightly below $100 two times after the previous NVDA article, and I have to be fair to say that I used these buying opportunities aggressively. Now I am 40% up, and I am confident that now it is good time to sell my NVDA holding once again because recent developments around the world are not positive at all, meaning that share price premiums are poised to shrink.

To start, a rapid spike of the RSI indicator from below 50 to 67 says quite a lot to me. The stock is close to the overbought zone, which means that we are very close to the share price pivot. A spoiler: Nvidia is also significantly overvalued at the moment. The "Overbought + Overvalued" formula almost never works well, especially when we speak about highly volatile stocks like NVDA.

Now let's speak about the broader context. The AI revolution remains hot, but there are some indications that tech giants are becoming more prudent in their data center buildout plans. The world's two largest cloud companies, Amazon ("AWS") and Microsoft ("Azure"), have been quietly pulling back some of their data center plans. I am not a person who loves to panic and who would say that the AI revolution is over. However, when the largest players are becoming less aggressive, it highly likely means that the peak data center buildout is over.

Besides the fact that the AI infrastructure is logically moving closer to a more mature stage, there are a few other signs that investors are unlikely to pay large premiums even for AI stars like Nvidia. Gold prices continue soaring and it is fair given the highly uncertain environment, with the notable uncertainty around various domains.

Despite the trade deal between the U.S. and China, I think it is very early to speak about the Fed's more dovish stance. Yesterday's CPI report showed that inflation increased once again, from 2.3% in April to 2.4% in May. The modest 0.1% CPI increase could have been nothing special in other circumstances, but we are currently in the reality where the effect of President Trump's tariffs is likely to hit within the next couple of months as tariffs usually have deferred effect. For example, Walmart's management warned the public that higher prices will be inevitable due to new tariffs. Of course, Trump told Walmart to "eat the tariffs," which was a good political move (in my humble opinion), but does not look feasible in the business reality given Walmart's inherently thin margins.

I do not like to speak about politics much, but developments in this field also affect stock prices. Therefore, I also have to mention that political situation within the United States also does not look perfect. There are quite notable anti-ICE protests across the country. There was a public fallout between Trump and Elon Musk (the world's richest person) via social networks. And there even were headlines that Trump would support arrest of California Governor Newsom.

All these developments might be just headlines, but they might significantly undermine investors' confidence regarding U.S. assets. Historical strength of the country's political institutions was one of the main reasons why investors from across the world invested in T-Bills and equities of American companies. However, if the political situation within the U.S. does not regain confidence with all these spooking headlines, investors are likely to continue fleeing U.S. markets.

Valuation Is Too Generous

Consensus forecasts are quite bullish as robust long-term EPS growth is projected. This forecast aligns with Nvidia's fundamental strength and favorable secular trends. Consequently, applying optimistic free cash flow growth assumptions into the DCF model appears to be fair.

Before drawing the FCF trajectory, the discount rate should be calculated. CAPM is a popular way to reliably calculate cost of equity, which is my favorite approach due to its simplicity and availability of the required variables. Under the CAPM approach, the discount rate is 10.8%.

The year 1 FCF assumption was calculated by multiplying the 37.3% margin by the projected FY 2026 revenue of $200 billion. The product is $74.6 billion, which is a reliable one to use as the year 1 FCF assumption. The projected free cash flow CAGR is 27.5%, which aligns with the projected industry CAGR.

The company's strong leadership position in AI processors makes me confident in a rather aggressive 4% growth rate into perpetuity. Nevertheless, even with optimistic long-term growth assumptions, the company's total fair value is around $2.47 trillion. This is 30% lower than a $3.5 trillion market cap, which is an extensively large premium in the current uncertain environment. Dividing a $2.47 trillion fair value cap by 24.4 billion shares outstanding gives me a fair share price of around $101.

Potential Drawbacks

"Past success does not guarantee future results" people say. I agree with this expression 100%, as there might be various unexpected positive events for Nvidia in our highly uncertain world. The U.S. might fall into a recession, which will highly likely force the Fed to become more dovish and start cutting interest rates more aggressively than it is currently expected. On the other hand, even with a significantly softer, 9% discount rate, Nvidia's fair value is still not higher than the current market cap.

What I am doing here with my Strong Sell rating for Nvidia, one of the most successful companies in history of America, is trying to time the market. This is inherently risky, and if you decide to do it, you better understand the extent of the risk of betting against companies like Nvidia.

Strong bullish sentiment of Wall Street analysts is a large positive factor that might keep the stock way higher than my $100 target price for longer. Therefore, I do not recommend to time the market if you are a risk-averse investor who plans to hold NVDA for 20 years ahead.

The Verdict

I liked that my previous NVDA timing and target price estimate were almost perfect, which allowed me to get nice returns. I think that the overvaluation has gone too far at the moment, which creates another good opportunity to ride this wave and buy this stellar company at circa $100 per share once again. Therefore, a Strong Sell it is.

data.title: Baidu: Not China's Google
data.description:
At first glance, Baidu's (NASDAQ:BIDU) (OTC:BAIDF) Q1 2025 earnings may seem like a positive inflection point. The company delivered a revenue beat, fueled by accelerating momentum in its AI cloud business, and surprised to the upside on non-GAAP profit. But digging one layer deeper, the picture becomes more complex: advertising revenue continues to shrink, while margins are fading and operating cash flow is declining.

In this note, I argue that Baidu is increasingly caught between its shrinking legacy cash engine and its overextended ambition to be an AI-first conglomerate. While Ernie and AI Cloud offer glimmers of traction, Baidu’s ability to monetize, scale, and differentiate in a winner-takes-most environment remains deeply uncertain. And as peers like Alibaba and Tencent double down on CAPEX to solidify their AI infrastructure stack, Baidu may be running out of time—and capital—to prove that its vision is more than just noise.

Baidu Reports Mixed Q1

Baidu reported results for Q1 2025, beating analyst consensus on topline and on earnings. During the period spanning from January through the end of March, Baidu generated revenues of RMB32.5 billion, up 3% YoY, and 5% above market expectations according to data collected by Refinitiv.

This topline beat was largely attributed to the robust performance of Baidu's AI cloud segment, which saw 42% YoY growth, reaching RMB6.7 billion and a clear acceleration in growth from 26% YoY growth in the previous quarter. Within cloud, the growth was driven by the wider adoption of Baidu's Ernie model, leading to higher cloud usage and triple-digit growth in Gen AI and foundation model-related revenue. However, Baidu's core online marketing (advertising) revenue saw a 6% YoY drop in revenues, to RMB16.0 billion. According to management commentary, the primary reasons for this downturn are ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty, as well as the company's strategic emphasis on upgrading its AI search functionality. While the AI search upgrade is enhancing user engagement, retention, and satisfaction, its monetization is still in the testing phase and is anticipated to negatively impact earnings in the short to medium term as Baidu prioritizes user experience over immediate revenue generation. Lastly, iQiyi, Baidu's video platform, saw its revenue fall by 9% YoY, to RMB7.2 billion due to a decrease in subscriptions.

Despite the overall revenue beat, Baidu's gross profit also declined by 8% YoY, with the margin narrowing due to a higher revenue contribution from the lower-margin cloud business. At the same time, non-GAAP net profit also fell 8% YoY, to RMB6.5 billion. Still, the profit was about 25-30% ahead of market estimates due to higher-than-expected cloud revenue and lower R&D expenses.

Trying To Do Too Much ...

Baidu's ambition span across autonomous driving/ robotaxis, electric vehicles, foundational AI models, cloud infrastructure, and streaming. While I appreciate the visionary zeal, I argue that Baidu faces a serious risk of strategic overextension.

Unlike Google, which possesses a dominant global search monopoly, an entrenched Android ecosystem, and massive cash flows from YouTube and cloud services, Baidu lacks similar financial and platform leverage. Its core business—Chinese internet search—is increasingly commoditized, while domestic ad revenues are under pressure from rivals like ByteDance and Alibaba. Indeed, weakness in Baidu core advertising business is expected to persist, with analysts forecasting an 8% decline in FY 2025 for this segment.

For example, the pursuit of self-driving robotaxis alone demands years of regulatory navigation, billions in R&D, and constant iterative hardware-software upgrade, an effort that strains even the most resource-rich companies like Apple and Tesla. Similarly, I am a sceptic of Baidu's AI ambitions, because building foundational models is not just expensive; it is becoming winner-take-most, and Baidu must compete with Alibaba's Qwen, Tencent's Hunyuan, Huawei's PanGu, and rising open-source models like DeepSeek. So far, the ERNIE models lack clear global traction and are not embedded into a ubiquitous ecosystem the way OpenAI models are integrated with Microsoft or Gemini with Google.

In essence, Baidu is acting like a conglomerate without the financial cushion or structural synergies to support such breadth. Its fragmented pursuit of scale in multiple deep-tech domains looks more like a frantic grab for future relevance than a coherent strategy built on sustainable competitive advantages.

... With Limited Capital

Google can afford a portfolio of moonshots because it’s backed by strong and growing free cash flow. Baidu, by contrast, faces shrinking revenue and tightening capital availability.

According to data collected by Refinitiv, Baidu's business is shrinking. For FY 2024, analysts now expect $18.7 billion of revenue.

Similarly, cash from operations is trending down: For FY 2025, analysts now expect $3.7 billion, down from $5+ billion estimated in late 2023.

Baidu's shrinking operating cash flow is concerning. Because ultimately, operating cash flow is needed to invest and sustain the competitive advantage against global giants like Google, Microsoft, and Meta, as well as domestic competitors such as Alibaba and Tencent.

Zooming in on the domestic perspective, I highlight that Alibaba's CAPEX guidance for fiscal year 2026 has surged—doubling since the end of December to nearly $15 billion, according to Refinitiv data. And notably, these spending expectations continue to rise sharply, underscoring the escalating investment intensity required to compete at the frontier of AI infrastructure and model development.

Similarly, Tencent is aggressively expanding CAPEX ambitions: For FY 2025, analysts now expect $14 billion worth of investments, up 100% from the same period estimated a year ago.

Investor Takeaway

I view Baidu's Q1 performance as mixed: While management can point to topline growth and AI-fueled optimism, margin erosion, and decelerating cash flows tell a more sobering truth: Baidu is stretching itself across too many capital- and talent-intensive frontiers without the economic foundation to support them.

In the new AI regime, success is increasingly reserved for firms with three interlocking advantages: platform ubiquity, developer mindshare, and deep financial reservoirs. Microsoft and Google possess all three. Alibaba and Tencent, at least domestically, do as well. Baidu? It commands none at scale. Its search platform is no longer the gateway to the Chinese web, its models are not standard in enterprise or consumer workflows, and its cash generation is moving in the wrong direction—with FY 2025 operating cash flow now expected at just $3.7 billion, down sharply from over $5 billion just a year ago.

There may still be optionality embedded in Baidu's longer-term narrative—especially if Ernie gains real traction or China accelerates state-backed support for domestic LLMs. But right now, investors are being asked to underwrite an increasingly stretched portfolio of "what ifs", while the core ads business is shrinking and losing margin power.
